content.problemStatement:
A positive integer N‚â•1N\ge 1N‚â•1 is given.
An NNN-uple (x1,x2,‚Ä¶,xN)(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_N)(x1‚Äã,x2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,xN‚Äã) of nonnegative integers is special if, after we remove all entries which are zero, its entries are nondecreasing. For example (0,2,0,0,4,0,4,5,0,0)(0, 2, 0, 0, 4, 0, 4, 5, 0, 0)(0,2,0,0,4,0,4,5,0,0) is special, but (3,2,0)(3, 2, 0)(3,2,0) and (0,1,3,0,0,1,0)(0, 1, 3, 0, 0, 1, 0)(0,1,3,0,0,1,0) are not.

One can show that there is a unique function Œº\muŒº defined on the NNN-uples of nonnegative integers such that, for any NNN-uple x=(x1,x2,‚Ä¶,xN)x=(x_1, x_2,\dots, x_N)x=(x1‚Äã,x2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,xN‚Äã)
\sum_{y \le x} \mu(y) =
\begin{cases}
    1 &\text{ifx is special} \\
    0 &\text{otherwise}
\end{cases},
where the sum ranges over all NNN-uples y=(y1,y2,‚Ä¶,yN)y=(y_1, y_2,\dots, y_N)y=(y1‚Äã,y2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,yN‚Äã) of nonnegative integers such that yi‚â§xiy_i\le x_iyi‚Äã‚â§xi‚Äã for all 1‚â§i‚â§N1\le i\le N1‚â§i‚â§N.

You are given x=(x1,x2,‚Ä¶,xN)x=(x_1,x_2,\dots, x_N)x=(x1‚Äã,x2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,xN‚Äã), determine whether Œº(x)\mu(x)Œº(x) is 000 or not.


Input Format

The first line will contain TTT, the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN, the size of the tuple considered.
The second line of each test case contains NNN integers x1,x2,‚Ä¶,xNx_1, x_2, \dots, x_Nx1‚Äã,x2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,xN‚Äã, the entries of the given NNN-uple.


Output Format
For each test case, print the string yes if Œº(x)=0\mu(x)=0Œº(x)=0 and the string no otherwise. You can print each letter in any case (upper or lower).
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 100‚Äâ000100\,000100000

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Explanation of the test cases 1, 2, 3:
For $N=1$, one can prove that $\mu((0)) = 1$ and $\mu((k))=0$ for all $k\ge 1$. 

 
 It holds $\mu((0)) = 1\not=0$, so the answer to testcase 1 is no. 
 It holds $\mu((1)) = 0$, so the answer to testcase 2 is yes. 
 It holds $\mu((10)) = 0$, so the answer to testcase 3 is yes. 


Explanation of testcases 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9:
For $N=2$, one can prove that $\mu((0,0))=1$, $\mu((k, k)) = 1$ and $\mu((k, k-1))=-1$ for all $k\ge 2$, and all other values of $\mu$ are $0$.
Let us check that the defining property of $\mu$ holds for $x=(2, 2)$ (notice that $x$ is special in this case):
$$ 1 = \sum_{y\le x} \mu(y) 
= \mu(0, 0) + \mu(0, 1) + \mu(0, 2) + 
\mu(1, 0) + \mu(1, 1) + \mu(1, 2)  + 
\mu(2, 0) + \mu(2, 1) + \mu(2, 2) $$
$$ = 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 - 1 + 1 = 1 .\hspace{13em}$$


 It holds $\mu((0, 1)) = 0$, so the answer to testcase 4 is yes. 
 It holds $\mu((1, 0)) = 0$, so the answer to testcase 5 is yes. 
 It holds $\mu((3, 2)) = -1$, so the answer to testcase 6 is no. 
 It holds $\mu((3, 3)) = 1$, so the answer to testcase 7 is no. 
 It holds $\mu((3, 4)) = 0$, so the answer to testcase 8 is yes. 
 It holds $\mu((10, 43)) = 0$, so the answer to testcase 9 is yes.
